ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP is the closest available category for pho-style beef noodle soup. No exact Pho Dac Biet code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of beef pho noodle soup, approximately 600 g, comprising rice noodles, beef oxtail slices, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Noodle Soup) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian noodle soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Pho Bo Dac Biet with oxtail in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite for Vietnamese beef pho (pho bo) using ingredient-level data for rice noodles, beef broth, oxtail, and mixed vegetables. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle soup category. The restaurant description states sugar-free and no added MSG; sodium estimated conservatively from fish sauce and broth components. Per-100g values reflect a typical mixed bowl composition (broth ~55%, noodles ~20%, meat ~15%, vegetables ~10%).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pho Bo Dac Biet. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on rice noodle-based soups in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Rice noodles (fresh/cooked) have a reported GI of approximately 40–53 depending on preparation. The broth and protein components lower the effective GI of the composite dish. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 40.8) / 100 ≈ 18.4, rounded to 18.5. Serving carbohydrate: 6.8 g/100g × 600 g serving = 40.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each 600 g bowl provides approximately 2280 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 18.5 per bowl and a low-to-moderate GI of 45, pho can be consumed by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions, prioritising the protein-rich broth and oxtail while reducing the noodle quantity to lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from oxtail and sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oxtail contributes saturated fat (approximately 4.8 g per bowl) and the broth contributes high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency and choose leaner beef cuts when available, while benefiting from the vegetable-rich broth base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Pho Noodle Soup (Special) — known in Vietnamese as Phở Bò Đặc Biệt — is a premium Vietnamese noodle soup dish served at Co Chung Restaurant in Singapore under the Revolution Phở brand. The dish is built on a slow-simmered bone broth made from oxtail and aromatic spices including star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and charred ginger and onion, which give the broth its characteristic deep amber colour and complex, slightly sweet, anise-forward flavour. The soup base is enriched by 12 types of fresh vegetables, making it nutritionally denser than standard pho. The dish is marketed as sugar-free and MSG-free, distinguishing it from many commercial pho preparations. It is served with flat rice noodles (bánh phở), premium oxtail slices, and traditional garnishes such as bean sprouts, fresh Thai basil, coriander, lime, and chilli. The texture profile combines silky noodles, gelatinous oxtail meat, and a clear but richly flavoured broth. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 62 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 2.1 g fat, and 6.8 g carbohydrate, making it a relatively low-calorie, protein-moderate dish. The primary clinical concern is sodium, with a full bowl estimated at approximately 2280 mg, which is significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for halal dietary requirements subject to restaurant certification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of beef pho?
What is the difference between pho and regular noodle soup?
How much protein is in pho?
Can I eat pho if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Pho Bo Dac Biet with oxtail exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for Vietnamese beef pho components (rice noodles, beef oxtail, bone broth, mixed vegetables) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle soup category data. GI is an analogous estimate from rice noodle soup data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level composites for Vietnamese beef pho (pho bo) and FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle soup analogues; no exact dish-level entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo. 2. Sodium estimate (380 mg/100g) is conservative and based on fish sauce and bone broth components; actual sodium may be higher depending on restaurant preparation. 3. The restaurant description states sugar-free and no added MSG; sugar_g is set to 0.5 reflecting naturally occurring sugars from vegetables and broth, not added sugar. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as oxtail and rice noodles contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; any trace ruminant trans fat is below detection threshold — confirmed zero by formulation analogy. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite for this dish type and is not expected to be a significant contributor. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source composite for this dish. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source composite for this dish. 8. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle soup data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Pho Bo Dac Biet was found. 9. Halal status is not confirmed for this specific restaurant; dietary_flags.is_halal is set to false pending restaurant certification. 10. Cuisine is classified as Vietnamese (origin) served in Singapore context; multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as the dish's origin language. 11. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for Pho Bo Dac Biet.

Verified trans_fat_g 0.0 justified in notes. Verified GI is numeric >0. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass.
